questions.scenario: A job starts on ubuntu-latest and immediately runs npm test. The log says package.json does not exist, although the file is present at the repository root. The workflow has no earlier steps. What is missing?

options.text: A job-level `defaults.run.working-directory` value that points to the repository root.
An artifact download from the workflow's build job so the workspace contains the source tree.
A checkout action that places the repository in the job workspace.
A reusable workflow that performs checkout before invoking the current test job.

questions.explanation: A runner receives job instructions, not an automatic copy of the repository. Checkout must populate `GITHUB_WORKSPACE` in this job before project commands can read `package.json`. A working-directory value only changes where commands look. Artifacts are not the normal source checkout, and a separate reusable-workflow job cannot populate this job's workspace. See [GitHub-hosted runners](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/manage-runners/github-hosted-runners/use-github-hosted-runners).

questions.scenario: A repository policy requires pull request checks to remain on GitHub-hosted runners. In that job, `npm ci` fails while compiling a PostgreSQL native module, and the log names a missing libpq header. Developers do not reproduce the failure because their laptops already have PostgreSQL development packages. What is the most reliable workflow fix?

options.text: Install the missing OS package before npm ci and pin the runtime with a setup action.
Run `npm ci` in a Node container whose image tag matches the developer laptops.
Cache the compiled native module from a successful Linux run and restore it before `npm ci`.
Move the job to a maintained self-hosted image that already contains libpq, without adding an explicit setup step.

questions.explanation: CI should declare both the OS library and language runtime before dependency installation. A generic Node container may still lack libpq headers. Restoring a compiled native module risks an ABI mismatch, while an undocumented self-hosted image hides the dependency instead of making the build reproducible. See [Customizing GitHub-hosted runners](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/manage-runners/github-hosted-runners/customize-runners).

questions.scenario: Integration tests must reach a database at a private VPC address and use a licensed test tool installed on controlled machines. Pull request unit tests need neither capability. Which runner split best limits access?

options.text: Run unit and integration jobs on the private pool, but isolate deployments on a second self-hosted runner group.
Use hosted runners for every job and establish a temporary VPN tunnel to the VPC for integration tests.
Package the licensed tool as an encrypted artifact and download it on hosted runners for the integration job.
Use hosted runners for ordinary checks and a narrowly labeled self-hosted pool only for the integration job.

questions.explanation: The integration job has explicit private-network and licensed-tool requirements, while ordinary checks do not. Sending unit tests into the private pool broadens code exposure. A VPN could solve reachability but not licensed-tool handling, and distributing the licensed binary may violate security and licensing constraints. See [GitHub self-hosted runners](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/concepts/runners/self-hosted-runners).

questions.scenario: A public repository allows fork pull requests to execute on a long-lived self-hosted runner inside the corporate network. Fork workflows receive no production secrets. What is still the primary risk?

options.text: The fork can change repository settings through the runner even when `GITHUB_TOKEN` has read-only permissions.
Untrusted pull request code can execute commands on the organization's machine and probe resources the runner can reach.
The fork can make GitHub-hosted jobs reuse the self-hosted runner's filesystem during later workflow runs.
The fork can read production secrets only if the workflow references those secrets in the pull request job.

questions.explanation: The primary boundary failure is executing attacker-controlled commands on a persistent organization host with internal reachability. Workspace persistence is another serious risk, but it is one consequence of the broader host compromise. Withholding GitHub secrets does not remove local credentials, network paths, or machine identity. See [GitHub self-hosted runner security](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/concepts/runners/self-hosted-runners).

questions.scenario: The team wants Node commands to run inside `node:22-bookworm`, needs PostgreSQL beside the tests with an explicit health check, and wants to update the Node and database images independently. Which workflow shape matches those requirements?

options.text: Use PostgreSQL as the job container and install Node inside that container before running the tests.
Run Node in a job container and start PostgreSQL in a separate job whose address is exported as an output.
Use node:22-bookworm as the job container and PostgreSQL as a service container with a health check.
Run both Node and PostgreSQL in a custom combined image so the job has one container lifecycle.

questions.explanation: The job container supplies the Node execution environment, while a service container supplies PostgreSQL on the same job network with an independent health check. Using PostgreSQL as the main container makes application tooling setup awkward. A database in another job is not a durable service for this job, and a combined image couples two lifecycles. See [Running jobs in a container](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/write-workflows/choose-where-workflows-run/run-jobs-in-a-container).

questions.scenario: The organization uses hosted runners for pull requests and private self-hosted runners for integration and deployment work. Which controls reduce risk across this mixed fleet?

options.text: Keep untrusted pull request checks on fresh hosted runners when private access is unnecessary.
Allow pull request jobs onto private runners when they request only labels that omit the word `prod`.
Give private jobs narrow labels that describe required network and tool capabilities.
Reuse persistent self-hosted workspaces for trusted and untrusted jobs, but remove repository files after each run.
Limit credentials and network access to the jobs and runner groups that need them.

questions.explanation: Fresh hosted runners reduce persistent-machine risk for ordinary untrusted checks. Narrow labels, runner groups, and access scopes make sensitive placement explicit. Label names are routing metadata, not a trust control. Deleting repository files alone does not remove processes, caches, tools, or other persistence left by untrusted code. See [Choosing a runner](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/write-workflows/choose-where-workflows-run/choose-the-runner-for-a-job).
